_meta.produced_by: Theia (T) — supersedes Pheme draft; Pheme draft backed up to CWST_industry_pheme_backup.json
_meta.note: Competitive landscape and addressable market for Daedalus. Every number is sourced and cited.

company.name: Casella Waste Systems, Inc.
company.description: Vertically integrated solid waste services company operating exclusively in the northeastern United States. Provides collection (residential, commercial, roll-off), transfer, landfill disposal, recycling, and organics processing. The 'vertically integrated' label is the core economic claim: CWST owns both front-end collection and back-end disposal infrastructure, closing the margin loop internally. Its business model is that of a regional consolidator: it acquires private haulers at below-consolidated margins, integrates them onto its routing and billing systems, raises prices to market rates, and routes acquired volumes to its own landfills ('internalization') to capture the full collection-to-disposal margin stack. Five consecutive years of double-digit revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted free cash flow growth through FY2025.
company.geography: Vermont, New Hampshire, Maine, Massachusetts, New York, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Delaware, Connecticut, West Virginia (since January 2026)
company.method_hint: EBITDA-multiple SOTP; four operating segments. Solid Waste (Eastern + Western + Mid-Atlantic) is the annuity with pricing-driven organic growth and acquisition-driven inorganic growth. Resource Solutions is commodity-sensitive and smaller. Each segment gets its own method and WACC.

economic_segments.description: Collection and disposal in Vermont, New Hampshire, Maine, and Massachusetts. Casella's original and most mature geography. Highest landfill internalization rates — Casella's owned Vermont and NH landfills serve this geography. Municipal and residential contracts predominate; pricing power is highest here because CWST has durable market position with very limited competition from national players.
economic_segments.margin_basis: Theia estimate: mature operations, highest internalization, lowest integration drag. Company discloses Mid-Atlantic entered at sub-consolidated margins (~18-20%); consolidated is ~23%; Eastern as the mature, highest-internalization segment must be above 23% to produce the consolidated average.
economic_segments.growth_profile: Low-to-mid single digit organic (pricing ~5% offset by ~-1% volume); modest bolt-on M&A
economic_segments.key_driver: CPI-plus pricing yield and route density

economic_segments.description: Collection, transfer, and disposal in New York State (Hudson Valley, Catskills, Western NY) and western Vermont. Includes the McKean Landfill in Pennsylvania — a rail-served facility accepting Northeast-exported waste from capacity-constrained markets. The Royal Carting and Welsh Sanitation acquisitions (New York and Connecticut, 2025) added collection volume being routed to CWST's NY landfills, driving 11.7% YoY landfill volume growth in Q3 2025.
economic_segments.margin_basis: Theia estimate: McKean rail-served third-party tons are incremental high-margin. Above consolidated average but below Eastern given newer NY acquisitions still integrating.
economic_segments.nature: mature-annuity with structural disposal pricing upside
economic_segments.growth_profile: Mid-single digit organic pricing; landfill volume growth from Northeast capacity displacement and internalization of acquired NY/CT collection volumes
economic_segments.key_driver: McKean Landfill third-party ton intake; internalization of Royal Carting/Welsh Sanitation volumes; disposal pricing in NY

economic_segments.description: Operations in Pennsylvania, Maryland, Delaware, and (since January 2026) West Virginia. Assembled via acquisition of GFL's PA/MD/DE assets (June 2023), LMR Disposal and Whitetail Disposal (2024, combined >$100M annualized revenue), and subsequent bolt-ons. This is Casella's newest and lowest-margin segment — billing system migration and route optimization still in progress. The margin headwind from Mid-Atlantic integration narrowed from ~100bps annually to ~10bps by Q3 2025. Star Waste Systems (closed April 1, 2026; ~$100M annualized revenue, Greater Boston) is the newest large addition.
economic_segments.margin_basis: Company-disclosed: acquired businesses enter at ~20% adj EBITDA margins. Mid-Atlantic is the integration-stage segment. Q1 2026 acquisitions diluted consolidated margin by 15bps — consistent with 20% entry margins.
economic_segments.nature: acquisition-integration converting toward annuity over 3-5 years
economic_segments.growth_profile: High revenue growth from M&A; margin catch-up is the primary value driver
economic_segments.key_driver: Integration execution: unified billing, route optimization, pricing normalization; $30M targeted G&A and synergy savings

sources.value: Mid-Atlantic headwind narrowed from 100bps annually to 10bps by Q3 2025
Acquired businesses enter at ~20% adj EBITDA margins; Q1 2026 acquisitions diluted 15bps consolidated

economic_segments.description: Materials recovery facilities (MRFs), organics processing, and commodity marketing. Revenue includes commodity-indexed sales of recovered fiber (OCC, newsprint, SMP), plastics, glass, and metals, plus processing/tipping fees. Commodity-price sensitive: OCC prices fell ~22% in Q1 2026, though contract evolution toward fee-for-service structures limited the net income impact to ~$1M. The Boston MRF is the flagship facility. Organics processing is growing under Massachusetts food waste mandate tailwind.
economic_segments.margin_basis: Company disclosed 270bps expansion in 2024. Below consolidated average. Commodity price exposure is the primary volatility source.
economic_segments.nature: cyclical-commodity with improving fee-based annuity component
economic_segments.growth_profile: Revenue sensitive to OCC/fiber prices; underlying fee-based volume growing 3-5% as municipal recycling contracts expand
economic_segments.key_driver: OCC / SMP commodity prices; processing fee rate; Boston MRF utilization

sources.value: $360.0M FY2025, up 9.1% YoY; 270bps margin expansion in 2024

consolidated_financials.note_on_gaap_vs_adjusted: Databundle reports GAAP EBITDA of $379M for FY2025 (margin 20.6%). Company's adjusted EBITDA of $422.8M adds back stock-based compensation and certain non-cash items. The 23.0% adjusted margin is the industry-standard benchmark for peer comparisons. All margin comparisons herein use adjusted EBITDA.

q1_2026.note: The 65bps base-business expansion vs. 15bps acquisition dilution decomposition shows the underlying organic margin improvement rate is solid.

fy2026_guidance_updated_april_30_2026.note: Raised from Feb 2026 initial guidance ($1.97-1.99B revenue, $455-465M adj EBITDA) following Star Waste acquisition and Q1 outperformance.

us_solid_waste_tam_usd_bn.basis: Waste Dive / Waste Business Journal document US waste industry revenue crossing $100B in 2024. Grand View Research ($156B, 2024) includes hazardous and specialized services at wider scope. Anythingresearch 'Solid Waste Collection' alone ~$85B. Theia uses $110B as midpoint for US non-hazardous solid waste collection, transfer, and disposal — the market CWST addresses.
us_solid_waste_tam_usd_bn.triangulation_note: Single-range estimate; sources span $85-156B depending on scope. $110B is Theia's midpoint for comparable-scope addressable market.

northeast_addressable_market.geography: CT, ME, MA, NH, NJ, NY, PA, RI, VT (NEWMOA 8-state) plus DE and WV (CWST recent entries)
northeast_addressable_market.basis: NEWMOA: 33.56M tons disposed in 8-state region in 2024. At Northeast average tipping fee of $84.44/ton (EREF 2024) plus collection economics (roughly 2x disposal revenue at blended rates), Theia estimates total Northeast solid waste market at ~$18-22B.
northeast_addressable_market.growth_driver: Disposal pricing rising faster than national average as Northeast capacity tightens. Average Northeast tipping fee up $8.52/ton in 2024 alone to $84.44/ton — highest US region.

sources.value: 33.56M tons disposed Northeast 2024; 23% at risk within 5 years
Northeast avg tipping fee $84.44/ton 2024, up $8.52/ton YoY — highest US region

competitors.name: Waste Management, Inc. (WM)
competitors.northeast_presence: Significant but less concentrated than CWST in Vermont, NH, western Maine
competitors.trajectory: Exceeded 30% adj EBITDA for first time in FY2025 (+150bps). Stericycle integration ongoing. Not a primary direct competitor to CWST's core rural New England markets.

competitors.name: Republic Services, Inc. (RSG)
competitors.northeast_presence: Present in major Northeast markets; less competitive with CWST in Northern New England rural markets. RSG's 2026 pricing yield guidance (3.2-3.7% total; 4.0-4.5% core) is below CWST's realized 5.1% in Q1 2026.
competitors.trajectory: 32% adj EBITDA in FY2025 (+90bps). Strong execution on Blue Polymer and RNG. The margin benchmark CWST is converging toward.

source.value: RSG FY2025 adj EBITDA 32%; 2026 yield guidance 3.2-3.7%

competitors.name: Waste Connections, Inc. (WCN)
competitors.northeast_presence: Limited. WCN targets markets where it can be dominant regional operator with limited WM/RSG competition — not a Northeast focus.
competitors.trajectory: Industry-leading margins (33.0% FY2025, +100bps YoY; targeting 33.3-33.4% in FY2026). WCN's secondary-market model is the closest structural analog to CWST's Northern New England position. WCN's margin trajectory is CWST's long-run ceiling reference.

source.value: WCN FY2025 adj EBITDA 33.0% +100bps; FY2026 target 33.3-33.4%

competitors.name: GFL Environmental Inc. (GFL)
competitors.northeast_presence: Divested PA/MD/DE solid waste assets to CWST in June 2023 (origin of CWST's Mid-Atlantic segment). Still present in NY and NJ.
competitors.trajectory: Targeting ~30.6% adj EBITDA in FY2026 (from 30.0% in 2025, 28.7% in 2024). Most relevant trading comp for CWST given similar acquisitive/leveraged profile at larger scale.

competitors.name: Private regional and local haulers (hundreds, Northeast US)
competitors.trajectory: Being consolidated at accelerating pace. M&A deal flow in environmental services was 25 deals YTD through mid-2025 vs. 16 in 2024.

source.value: PE bolt-on acquisitions at 5-6x; route density integration expands margins 14% to 28%

structural_reasons_for_gap: High-pace acquisition mode: new businesses enter at ~20% adj EBITDA margins, diluting the consolidated figure. Q1 2026 base business expanded 65bps while new acquisitions added 15bps dilution, netting to +50bps. Underlying organic margin improvement rate is ~65bps/quarter.
Mid-Atlantic integration still underway — the largest integration project in CWST history, narrowed from 100bps headwind to 10bps by Q3 2025.
G&A overhead proportionally higher in growth mode: $30M targeted savings represent ~145bps of margin at FY2026E revenue scale.
Landfill internalization still in progress — as Mid-Atlantic and NY/CT acquired volumes route to CWST landfills, 50-75% incremental margin per ton flows through.
margin_gap_peer_table.convergence_rate_evidence: Q1 2026 base business organic: +65bps/quarter (~200-260bps/year). Mid-Atlantic headwind near zero. G&A: $30M targeted = ~145bps. Internalization: each 1% point of additional internalization adds ~50-75bps. A reasonable 3-year accumulation puts CWST at 25-27% by FY2028-FY2029. Reaching WCN's 33% requires either much longer integration (8-10+ years) or a structural step-change management has not guided toward.
margin_gap_peer_table.ceiling_reference: WCN at 33.0% is the best-in-class secondary-market analog. GFL at 30.0% is the closest acquired-and-integrated analog. RSG at 32.0% is the national-scale peer. A realistic long-run CWST ceiling is 28-30% assuming continued acquisitions and successful integration — not 33%.

the_share_dynamic.summary: The defining share dynamic in US solid waste is consolidation. CWST is not taking share from WM or RSG in head-to-head competition — it is acquiring private haulers that are too small for national players to pursue (<$50-100M revenue each) and integrating them onto its disposal network. The secondary dynamic is landfill internalization: as CWST acquires a collection business, it reroutes volumes to its own landfills, converting third-party tipping fee cost into captive margin.
the_share_dynamic.organic_volume_trend: Negative to flat in mature markets. Q4 2025 organic solid waste volume was -0.9% YoY. Northeast population growth is near zero; organic MSW volume growth is structurally 0-1% for the region. Pricing, not volume, is the organic growth engine.
the_share_dynamic.pricing_trend: CWST delivering 4.9-5.1% solid waste pricing vs. WM/RSG 3.2-4.5% core pricing yield. CWST's premium reflects concentrated market positions in tight-capacity Northeast markets.

landfill_capacity_advantage.summary: Northeast landfill capacity is in structural decline. NEWMOA 2024: 30% capacity loss since 2015; 23% of remaining in-region capacity (7.6M tons/year) at risk of closure within 5 years. Even with approved expansions (Juniper Ridge, Seneca Meadows), 5.2M tons/year will need a new destination before end of decade. CWST's McKean (rail-served, PA), Vermont, and New York landfills are positioned as receiving sites for this displaced volume.
landfill_capacity_advantage.tipping_fee_context: Northeast average $84.44/ton in 2024 (up $8.52/ton from 2023) — highest US region and accelerating. Sullivan County NY raised C&D tipping fees to $150/ton in January 2026.

sources.value: 30% capacity loss since 2015; 23% at risk within 5 years; 5.2M tons still needing destination even with approved expansions

moat.primary: Landfill permits in the Northeast. Constructing a new landfill near Northeast population centers is effectively impossible — documented by 30% capacity loss since 2015 (NEWMOA 2024). CWST's permitted landfill positions in Vermont, New York, Pennsylvania, and Maine are irreplaceable assets conferring captive disposal, third-party volume intake capacity, and structural pricing leverage as the region's capacity tightens.
moat.secondary: Route density and geographic monopolies in Northern New England. In Vermont, NH, and western Maine, CWST is the only integrated hauler of meaningful scale. Route density creates a fixed-cost advantage — the marginal cost of adding a stop on an existing route is very low.
moat.tertiary: Municipal contracts. Long-term (5-10 year) residential collection contracts with CPI-linked escalators provide an annuity base with high switching costs for municipalities.

moat_strength.disposal_landfill: STRONG — structural supply constraint, irreplaceable assets
moat_strength.collection_mature_markets: MODERATE-STRONG — route density, municipal contracts, switching costs in VT/NH/ME
moat_strength.collection_mid_atlantic: MODERATE — newer market, building density, WM/RSG present
moat_strength.resource_solutions: WEAK — commodity exposure, multiple competitors for recycling contracts

five_forces.supplier_power: LOW. Labor is the key input; manageable at current wage levels. Fuel is partially hedged. Equipment from competitive OEMs.
five_forces.buyer_power: LOW in core New England markets (structural monopoly positions, limited alternatives). MODERATE for large commercial accounts. LOW for municipal contracts (long-term, sticky).
five_forces.competitive_rivalry: LOW in Vermont, NH, Maine (CWST dominant). MODERATE in New York and Mid-Atlantic (WM/RSG present, commercial account competition).
five_forces.threat_of_substitutes: VERY LOW. Waste must be collected and disposed of. No technology substitutes for physical collection and disposal.
five_forces.threat_of_entry: VERY LOW for integrated operators. Landfill permitting is functionally closed in the Northeast. New hauler-only entrants lack captive disposal — paying market-rate tipping fees to CWST creates a structural cost disadvantage.

named_acquisitions_recent.name: GFL Environmental PA/MD/DE assets
named_acquisitions_recent.significance: Created Mid-Atlantic segment; largest deal in CWST history

named_acquisitions_recent.name: LMR Disposal LLC + Whitetail Disposal Inc (PA/MD)
Royal Carting / Welsh Sanitation (NY/CT)
named_acquisitions_recent.significance: NY/CT market; volumes routed to CWST NY landfills for internalization benefit

named_acquisitions_recent.name: Star Waste Systems (Greater Boston, MA)
named_acquisitions_recent.significance: Largest recent acquisition. Boston is the highest-pricing MSA in the Northeast. First major Greater Boston presence for CWST.
